A.name: The Missed-Call Leak
A.desc: Booking-process leak angle. Emergency wildlife caller is already panicking — your site loses them at the booking button before the phone even rings. $2K-$6K/week anchor. Tease third issue. Dual-path close.

beats.title: Booking Process Leak
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with CritterClick — I review wildlife removal sites for a living. I pulled up your site, and your booking process is costing you roughly $2,000 to $6,000 a week in missed jobs. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
responses: What do you mean costing me?
beats.followup: Got it — here's what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where the jobs are going.
beats.note: Calling the business, not a person. No name tokens. No apology hedges. Dollar anchor = 5-10 missed jobs/wk × $400-$600 avg wildlife removal job.

beats.title: Emergency Caller, 3-Second Window
beats.line: Here's the deal — when a homeowner hears something in the attic at night, they Google, tap the first ad, and decide in about 3 seconds whether they're calling you or moving on. Wildlife removal is emergency-service buying — that caller will hire the first shop that makes it easy to reach them. When was the last time someone on your team pulled up your site on a phone and tried to call or book right from it?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's the most common answer. The booking button is just the first leak. The bigger ones come after.
beats.note: Plain English. Emergency-service mindset. Ends with a question they can't confidently answer.

beats.title: The Booking Button Costs You the Job
beats.line: Here's what's actually happening. The homeowner taps your ad, counts to three, and your phone number isn't front and center — so they back out and tap the next shop. The next shop picks up, gets the job, and gets paid $400 to $600 by 9 in the morning. The ones who do find your number land on a booking process that asks them to fill out a form before they've even said what's in their attic. By the time they figure it out, they've already called two other shops. That's the $2,000 to $6,000 a week. Getting your phone number visible and making it easy to book — those are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your site that costs more than both of those combined. I'd rather show you that one with your page open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Tease the third issue. Do NOT reveal it on the call. The reveal is the audit. Dollar-anchor the competitor steal.

beats.title: 100+ Wildlife Sites, Same 3 Leaks
beats.line: I've done this on over 100 wildlife removal sites — same 10-point checklist, same booking process, same three leaks every time. The shops that fix the top two see calls climb in the first week. The ones that fix all three usually double their booked jobs inside 30 days.
responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition.

beats.title: Free $1,500 Audit
beats.line: Here's what I want to do. I run a $1,500 conversion audit — and I'd like to do yours free. Thirty minutes. Your live site on screen. Every leak named in plain English no agency would dare use, with dollar-anchored estimates per leak — just the numbers, in your numbers. You walk away with a sequenced fix-list ranked by conversion impact and effort to ship — quick wins first. Yours to keep whether you hire me to ship it or not. Not vibes — math.
beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Verbatim critterclick.com language throughout. $1,500 free. One-week plan. All fixes shipped at once.

beats.title: Calendar or Sample
beats.line: Here's how this works. I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2 open — pick one and I'll send the calendar invite. Or, if you'd rather see what this looks like first, give me your email and I'll send over a sample audit I did for another wildlife removal shop — full fix-list, dollar numbers, the whole thing. Which one works for you?
beats.followup: Done — calendar invite or sample audit hits your inbox in 60 seconds.
beats.note: Dual-path close. Booked call OR sample audit. Either is a win.

B.name: Midnight Emergency Steal
B.desc: Homeowner hears scratching at 11pm. Taps your ad, your site doesn't make it easy to call or book. They tap the next ad. The next shop gets the $600 job by 9am. Steal-frame audit. $2K-$6K/week anchor. Dual-path close.

beats.title: Midnight Steal Frame
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with CritterClick — I review wildlife removal sites for a living. Quick question — do you know how many homeowners hear something scratching in their attic at night, tap your ad, and book the next shop instead? Because on your site right now, that's costing you $2,000 to $6,000 a week. Who's the right person to talk to about this?
responses: What do you mean costing me?
beats.followup: Got it — here's what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where those jobs are going.
beats.note: Question-first hook lands the steal frame immediately. No name tokens. No apology hedges.

beats.title: 11pm Panic Tap
beats.line: Here's what I did — I pulled up your site on my phone the way a panicked homeowner would at 11 at night. Your phone number wasn't front and center. There was no obvious way to book right from the page. Emergency-service buyers don't scroll — they tap, they look for a number, and if they don't see it in 3 seconds, they're already on the next shop's site. When's the last time anyone on your team actually tested that on a phone?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's what most operators say. The phone number is just the first leak. The bigger ones come after.
beats.note: Side-by-side reframe makes the issue concrete. Ends with a question they can't confidently answer.

beats.title: Next Shop Gets the Morning Job
beats.line: Here's what's actually happening. The homeowner taps your ad at 11pm, can't find your number fast enough, and taps the next shop. The next shop has a phone number right at the top. They pick up — or they have a callback request button that works. The next shop gets the $600 job by 9 in the morning. You never even showed up on that homeowner's screen for more than 3 seconds. The ones who do find your number hit a booking process that slows them down before they've decided anything. They open two more tabs and forget you. That's the $2,000 to $6,000 a week. Fixing the phone visibility and the booking path — those are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your site that costs more than both of those combined. I'd rather show you that one with your page open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Tease the third issue. Do NOT reveal it on the call. Steal-frame: competitor gets the morning job.

beats.title: 100+ Wildlife Sites Audited
beats.line: I've audited over 100 wildlife removal sites — same 10-point checklist, same booking process, same three leaks every time. The shops that fix the top two see calls climb in the first week. The ones that fix all three usually double their booked jobs inside 30 days.
responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition.

beats.title: Flip the Steal — Free $1,500 Audit
beats.line: Here's what I want to do. I run a $1,500 conversion audit — and I'd like to do yours free. Thirty minutes. Your live site on screen. I show you every leak, attach a dollar number to each one — just the numbers, in your numbers — and hand you a sequenced fix-list to flip the steal, so the next time a homeowner's panicking at midnight and taps your ad, yours is the site that closes the call. Yours to keep whether you hire me to ship it or not. Not vibes — math.
beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Steal-frame hook ties audit back to Beat 3. Dollar-anchored. One-week plan.

beats.title: Calendar or Sample
beats.line: Here's how this works. I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2 open — pick one and I'll send the calendar invite, and I'll get your site flipped from the one losing the midnight call to the one closing it. Or, if you'd rather see what this looks like first, give me your email and I'll send over a sample audit I did for another wildlife removal shop.
beats.followup: Done — calendar invite or sample audit hits your inbox in 60 seconds.
beats.note: Dual-path close. Steal-frame tilt on calendar path. Either choice is a win.

C.name: 3-Issue Reveal
C.desc: Names two of three issues outright in plain English (top of page + how they actually book), teases the third for the audit. $2K-$6K/week anchor. Dual-path close.

beats.title: Three Issues Frame
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with CritterClick — I review wildlife removal sites for a living. I pulled up your site and I'm seeing three specific issues on your booking process that together are costing you roughly $2,000 to $6,000 a week in missed jobs. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
beats.followup: Got it — here's what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where the jobs are going.
beats.note: Calling the business, not a person. Three-issues hook sets up the reveal.

beats.title: Three Stuck Spots
beats.line: Here's the deal — every wildlife removal site I look at has three spots where homeowners get stuck between tapping the ad and actually calling or booking: the top of the page, the part where they actually try to reach you, and one more issue most operators never notice. On your site, all three are losing you jobs. For a panicked homeowner at 11 at night, if your number isn't right there at the top, they're already gone.
beats.followup: Yeah — that's the most common reaction. The reveal is in the audit.
beats.note: Plain-English version of CRO failure stack. No jargon. Tee up the reveal in Beat 3.

beats.title: Walk Through Two Of Three
beats.line: So here's what's actually happening. The top of your page doesn't have a phone number front and center, and there's no urgency — no reason for a panicked homeowner to call right now instead of scrolling. So they back out and tap the next shop. The ones who stay long enough hit how they actually try to reach you — you're asking them to fill out a form before they've even said what's in their attic. That's a wall. They open two more tabs and forget you. That's the $2,000 to $6,000 a week. Those two are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your site that costs more than both of those combined. I'd rather show you that one with your page open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Reveal two issues plain. Tease third. Do NOT name the third on the call — the reveal is the audit.

responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
